af_score_components.notes: This is an MCP HOST (client application), NOT an MCP server — it does not expose tools via MCP. Evaluated as an MCP consumer tool. No auth required for the app itself. Built-in Bash tool gives LLMs shell access — significant security implication. MCP server authentication support is marked as unstable. Electron and Tauri variants available across Win/Mac/Linux. macOS requires manual Python and Node.js installation.

security_score_components.security_notes: Local desktop app with HTTPS for LLM API calls. LLM API keys stored in local configuration — no centralized secret management. Built-in Bash tool is a significant security risk for shell access from LLMs. MCP server auth support unstable. Electron/Tauri desktop apps have different security profiles. No compliance certifications.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Desktop application with no SLA. v0.14.1 — moderate maturity for a desktop app. MCP server auth support instability creates reliability concerns. No documented breaking change policy. Desktop must be running for operation. Dependency on installed Python/Node.js on macOS adds setup fragility.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server with good quality (78). Cabinet system for scoping docs to your tech stack is the key differentiator from Context7. Requires signup and API key even on free tier. 1,000 requests/month free limit may constrain heavy development use. Documentation accuracy depends on community curation. v2.0.1 — reasonably mature. Very similar to Context7 — evaluate both.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced on hosted MCP endpoint. API key + OAuth2 for remote connections. No scope granularity on API keys. No formal compliance certifications listed. API key via DOCFORK_API_KEY environment variable. Cabinet system provides logical query scoping but is not a security boundary. v2.0.1 with unknown dependency audit status.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Hosted SaaS MCP with no explicitly published SLA. v2.0.1 — stable for documented features. No formal breaking change policy. Full GET idempotency on documentation queries. 1,000 request/month free limit is a hard reliability boundary for development use. Community curation means doc freshness varies by library.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. OpenAPI spec available. PATs (Personal Access Tokens) recommended over passwords — read-only tokens useful for monitoring agents. Rate limits can silently break agent workflows — always authenticate even for public image pulls. v1 and v2 APIs have different auth flows — use v2 (hub.docker.com/v2). Image tags are mutable — use digest for stable reference. No retry guidance documented.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. PATs with read-only or read-write permission — no per-resource or per-repository scoping. 2FA requires PAT usage. Docker Hub infrastructure maintained by Docker Inc. PAT via environment variable recommended. No compliance certifications explicitly listed. Rate limits affect security of public image pulls without auth.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Managed SaaS with status page. v2 API is stable and preferred. Migration from v1 to v2 documented. Cursor-based pagination. Partial idempotency — DELETE and PUT operations are safe to retry. Rate limits can silently break workflows when exceeded. Image tag mutability requires using digests for reliable image references.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Excellent docs with multi-language SDK. Simple API key. Monthly document limits — production agents must track consumption. External resources must be publicly accessible from DocRaptor's servers — local/private URLs fail silently. JavaScript NOT executed — must server-side render content. Test mode with hardcoded key produces watermarked PDFs. Base64-encode images for reliability.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key in request body or Basic Auth. No scope granularity. GDPR compliant. Multi-language SDK maintained. Test API key is a hardcoded string ('YOUR_API_KEY_HERE') — clear separation of test and production modes. External resource fetching from DocRaptor's servers may expose internal URLs.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Managed SaaS with SLA. Stable versioning with multi-language SDK parity. Retry guidance documented. Full idempotency on PDF generation (same HTML + same params = same PDF). Monthly document limits create hard availability boundaries for high-volume agents. PDF/A and PDF/X compliance modes add processing reliability for regulated documents.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. OpenAPI spec available. JWT Service Account flow preferred for agents (no user interaction). JWT requires RSA private key management — secure key storage is critical. Envelopes in 'sent' status cannot be modified — must void and resend. Webhook (Connect) requires publicly accessible HTTPS endpoint. Sandbox and production use different base URLs and accounts. Rate limits are per-account across all integrations.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. JWT + OAuth2 with granular permission scopes. SOC1/SOC2/GDPR/HIPAA/ISO27001/eIDAS/ESIGN — gold-standard compliance for legally binding document platform. RSA private key management for JWT requires HSM or secrets manager integration. Sandbox/production separation prevents accidental signature sends. Legally binding document audit trails require careful secret handling.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Enterprise SaaS with contractual SLA and status page. Stable versioned API. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. No idempotency on envelope creation — retrying creates duplicate envelopes. Rate limits are per-account and shared across integrations — plan for concurrent agent usage. Webhook HMAC verification required for production security.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server with good quality (78). Service tokens scoped per project/config are ideal for agents — minimal permissions by design. Secret download returns all secrets at once — not per-key fetch. Service tokens must match the correct project/environment combination. Full idempotency on secret reads. Webhooks for secret changes require HTTPS tunnel in local dev. Excellent secrets management solution for agent workflows.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Service tokens scoped to single project/config with read-only or read-write permissions — excellent least-privilege design. SOC2/GDPR. Service tokens via environment variables (appropriate given this is a secrets manager). Full audit trail on secret access. Config-level scope granularity prevents cross-project secret access. Excellent security architecture for a secrets manager.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Managed SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable versioning. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. Full idempotency on secret reads. MCP server is newer — test thoroughly before production use. Secret propagation timing varies — understand when immediate propagation is needed vs. eventual consistency. Excellent overall reliability posture.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. API documentation behind customer portal (active subscription required). Rate limits not publicly documented. OAuth2 + API key with scope-based access controls. Evidence collection primarily driven by integrations, not direct API uploads. Control and framework terminology varies between Drata versions. Webhook event catalog growing — check current docs. Enterprise-only pricing.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. OAuth2 + API keys with scope-based access controls. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR — comprehensive compliance for a compliance automation platform. Scoped access controls limit which compliance resources are accessible. OAuth2 tokens and API keys via environment variables. Enterprise-grade security appropriate for handling compliance evidence and audit data.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Enterprise SaaS with SLA. Stable API versioning. Good backward compatibility. No idempotency on evidence uploads. No retry guidance. Growing webhook event catalog requires staying current with docs. Rate limits undocumented — contact support for enterprise limits. Documentation behind customer portal limits pre-integration evaluation.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server with decent quality (72). Requires initial drift_scan before other tools produce results. ~70 tools via drift_tool may overwhelm agent context — use drift_discover to filter. V2 is a major rewrite of v1 (deprecated). README is minimal — real docs are in drift v2/docs/. No LICENSE file found. Supabase integration suggests optional cloud sync. Docker + stdio/HTTP transport available. Rust/NAPI-RS backend for performance.

security_score_components.security_notes: Local tool with no network exposure by default. No authentication required. No credentials stored. Supabase cloud integration would change the security posture. Unknown version and unclear license (no LICENSE file) are supply-chain concerns. Rust backend provides memory safety. No compliance certifications. HTTP transport via Docker adds potential network exposure.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted with no SLA. Unknown version, v2 rewrite in active development — expect instability. v1 deprecated with no migration path. Requires initial drift_scan to populate analysis results. Partial idempotency on read tools. 70+ tools creates operational complexity. No documented breaking change policy. Early-stage tool with significant potential but production risk.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Good documentation with multi-language SDK. OAuth2 with granular scopes is secure but requires refresh token management for long-running agents. Large file uploads require chunked upload sessions — not a single PUT. Paths are case-insensitive but case-preserving. Shared link permissions default to viewer — must explicitly set edit. Business accounts have complex namespace/team space handling.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. OAuth2 with granular permission scopes (files.content.read, files.content.write, sharing.write, etc.). Short-lived access tokens with refresh token rotation. SOC2/GDPR/HIPAA/ISO27001 — comprehensive compliance for cloud file storage. Tokens managed via OAuth flow with secure storage. Granular scopes enable least-privilege access per agent use case.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Consumer/Business SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable versioning with multi-language SDK parity. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. Cursor pagination. OAuth access token expiry requires refresh logic for long-running agents. Chunked upload sessions handle large file reliability. Partial idempotency on file operations.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Purpose-built for AI agent code execution — excellent for code interpreter loops. Simple E2B_API_KEY with no OAuth. Sandboxes pause (not destroy) on keepalive timeout — resume via sandbox_id. Package installs not cached — use custom templates for dependency-heavy workflows. Concurrent sandbox limits require pool/semaphore patterns. Network egress from sandboxes enabled by default — egress control for untrusted code is important.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. VM-level isolation provides strong security boundary for code execution. Network egress from sandboxes enabled by default — important consideration when running untrusted AI-generated code. E2B_API_KEY via environment variable. Purpose-built security model for sandboxed code execution. No formal compliance certifications listed.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Managed SaaS with status page. Stable API versioning. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. Sandbox ID enables resume on pause rather than recreation. Custom templates needed for reliable dependency installation. 24-hour max session and concurrent sandbox limits require planning. Package installation in sandbox not cached — affects startup latency for dependency-heavy workloads.
